title: To amend the Aquifer Recharge Flexibility Act to clarify a provision relating to conveyances for aquifer recharge purposes.
plain_summary: This bill makes it easier for water agencies to use their existing permits to store water underground in aquifers on federal land. Under current law, permit holders can already do this for themselves without getting new permission from the government. The new bill expands this to let permit holders also help states, tribes, and public agencies store water underground using the same permits. The permit holders must give the Bureau of Land Management thirty days notice before doing this, and they will not have to pay extra fees unless they are making a profit from storing the water.
last_action_text: Received in the Senate and Read twice and referred to the Committee on Energy and Natural Resources.

sections.title: Section 1 Conveyance for Aquifer Recharge Purposes
sections.text: This section changes the Aquifer Recharge Flexibility Act to make it easier for water agencies to use existing permits and rights to store water underground. The holder of a permit or right-of-way can now let states, local governments, tribal governments, or public agencies use their existing permits to transport and store water underground in aquifers. This is called aquifer recharge. They do not need to get new permission from the Secretary of the Interior for this use. The law says this use does not count as expanding or changing the original permit. However, the permit holder must give the Bureau of Land Management at least thirty days written notice before allowing this use. The notice must include who will be using the permit, which specific permit will be used, details about how it will be used for aquifer recharge, and a copy of the agreement between the permit holder and the water agency. The section also makes clear that this law does not excuse anyone from following federal laws and Bureau policies. It also does not give anyone permission to build new structures or expand existing ones. Finally, the section makes small word changes throughout the law to make it clearer.
